title: Why California’s carbon manure math doesn’t add up
gist: California’s dairy-manure biogas program under the Low Carbon Fuel Standard mis-weighs methane vs CO2, letting fuel producers trade short-lived methane cuts for permanent CO2 emissions—an offset accounting flaw the author argues will lock in more long-run warming.

insights.claim: California's Low Carbon Fuel Standard assumes methane exerts ~25x the warming of CO2 over 100 years, but methane breaks down within a couple of decades while CO2 persists for centuries to millennia, so the program trades short-term warming reductions for near-permanent warming.
insights.speaker: James Temple
insights.so_what: Signals regulatory/reputational risk for LCFS-credit-dependent biogas and RNG business models if the GWP accounting is revised.
insights.quote: So, in effect, the state has created a system that reduces short-term warming at the cost of increasing all-but-permanent warming.
insights.locator: But there’s a problem with this carbon math

insights.claim: Under California's program, adding one average biogas-powered vehicle generates enough LCFS credits to cover the deficits of 26 comparable gasoline vehicles, per UC Berkeley economist Aaron Smith.
insights.speaker: James Temple
insights.so_what: Quantifies the outsized subsidy leverage of dairy biogas credits—a lucrative but fragile arbitrage if credit ratios are tightened.
insights.quote: adding one average biogas-powered vehicle to the fleet would produce enough LCFS credits to cover the deficits incurred by 26 similar gasoline-powered vehicles
insights.locator: In fact, methane is so much more powerful

insights.claim: California regulators decided in 2024 to extend parts of the dairy digester program beyond 2050, and a state air resources board proposal could direct millions more to dairy farmers while easing restrictions on major greenhouse-gas producers.
insights.speaker: James Temple
insights.so_what: Near-term tailwind for dairy digester economics, but the extension is politically and scientifically contested—monitor for reversal.

insights.claim: The dairy manure program became wildly popular purely because the subsidies are extremely lucrative, illustrating how convoluted incentive schemes swap climate responsibilities between parties and regions rather than forcing direct emission cuts.
insights.speaker: James Temple
insights.so_what: Frames offset-driven revenue as subsidy-dependent, not fundamentals-driven—valuation risk if incentives shift.

insights.claim: Carbon offsetting and trading schemes repeatedly and dramatically overstate the emissions reductions actually achieved in the atmosphere, making them an unreliable substitute for direct decarbonization.
insights.speaker: James Temple
insights.so_what: Investor lens for scrutinizing any offset-based ESG or carbon-credit revenue stream.
insights.locator: As studies have shown again and again

insights.claim: Every sector must fully decarbonize over the coming decades, so allowing one industry to make paper progress by paying another to cut emissions is increasingly untenable as all industries race to net zero.
insights.speaker: James Temple
insights.so_what: Points to eventual policy shift from tradable offsets toward direct mandates—repricing risk for offset-reliant assets.
insights.locator: The problem I keep returning to
